output:
Model-Mechanism-Conflated Correctness in Planted-Ground-Truth XAI Benchmarks

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

Post-hoc feature-attribution explainability (XAI) for NLP is migrating from surrogate faithfulness criteria toward CORRECTNESS: scoring an explanation against a controlled ground truth of which input features are genuinely class-relevant. The suppressor-variable lineage [1,2,3] gives correctness a precise statistical definition, and GECOBench [5] operationalizes it for pre-trained language models by PLANTING gender ground truth -- editing Wikipedia sentences (drawn from the top-100 Gutenberg books) so that the gender-defining tokens become, by construction, the only class-relevant features for a binary gender-classification task. BERT [17] is fine-tuned at varying depths and each attribution method -- Saliency [14], Integrated Gradients [11], LRP [12], SHAP [13], GradxInput/DeepLIFT [15], pattern-based [16] -- is scored by the fraction of attribution mass on planted tokens. This is the first controlled-ground-truth correctness benchmark for explanations of language models. However, the evaluation imports a hidden assumption: that the model actually USES only the planted tokens. Binary male/female labeling and gender-swapping are inherited dataset-construction devices that give a controlled, invertible ground truth; they are not an endorsement of binary gender, and "gender tokens" here denote lexical/grammatical markers (pronouns, gendered names/nouns), not identity. This proposal diagnoses the benchmark's correctness estimator, naming the conflation Model-Mechanism-Conflated Correctness (MMCC), and resolves it by conditioning the score on a causally-measured per-model ground-truth-usage denominator.

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does the GECOBench explanation-correctness scalar -- attribution mass on human-planted ground-truth gender tokens -- suffer from Model-Mechanism-Conflated Correctness (MMCC), whereby it entangles explainer-attributable error, model-mechanism misalignment (the fine-tuned LM causally relying on co-referent, contextual, positional, or subword cues rather than only the planted tokens, so that a faithful explanation is scored incorrect), and tokenizer/word-aggregation error while averaging over subject type, context distance, sentence length, and fine-tuning depth, such that promoting it to a per-construction, estimator-marginalized correctness profile denominated by causally-measured per-model ground-truth-token usage separates these sources and provably collapses back to the original scalar when measured usage equals one for every planted token, per-construction weights are uniform, a single fixed readout is used, and scoring is word-level with no subword split?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

MMCC matters first because it can invert the practical conclusions drawn from correctness leaderboards. GECOBench [5] and its lineage [1,2,3,4] produce rankings of attribution methods (Integrated Gradients [11], LRP [12], SHAP [13], Saliency [14], GradxInput/DeepLIFT [15], pattern-based [16]) by mass-on-planted-tokens. If, in a given construction, the fine-tuned model genuinely relies on co-referent or contextual cues rather than the planted token -- and fine-tuned language models are well documented to exploit shortcuts and contextual cues rather than the intended signal (shortcut/Clever-Hans learning [18]) -- then the method that scores HIGHEST is the one that most over-attributes to the planted gold (the LEAST faithful method to that model), while a faithful method that follows the model's true shortcut is ranked lowest. Causal-mediation analysis of gender behavior in Transformers [9] shows that gender effects are mediated by co-referent pronouns, attention heads, and distributed context, not by a single subject token. Under MMCC the leaderboard is partly a ranking of model-shortcut prevalence per construction, not of explainer quality [conjectured->measured]. A practitioner who selects an attribution tool from such a leaderboard can therefore pick the wrong tool: they optimize for agreement with a human-planted prior rather than for fidelity to the model they actually need to debug.

The same confound corrupts faithfulness verdicts in both directions. Jacovi and Goldberg [6] distinguish faithfulness (agreement with the model's true reasoning) from plausibility (agreement with what a human expects). A faithful method gets a false "incorrect" verdict whenever the model uses non-planted cues; an unfaithful method gets a false "correct" verdict whenever its bias toward the salient planted token happens to match the gold. Sanity checks [7] showed that some saliency maps behave like edge detectors, and ROAR [8] found many methods do not beat random once information leakage is controlled -- yet even these sophisticated diagnostics cannot separate "the explanation is unfaithful" from "the model's mechanism diverges from the human gold" because they never plant token-level ground truth and denominate by per-token model usage.

Crucially, MMCC is not gender-specific. The defect lives in the evaluation template, not in the gender content: the contribution is gender-agnostic and conditions a planted-ground-truth correctness score on causally-measured per-model token usage, transferring unchanged to any swap-planted or template-planted benchmark (sentiment, NLI, toxicity). Any finding that a model leans on context, pronoun, or subword shortcuts is reported as a model/benchmark artifact surfaced by the profile, not as a claim about real-world gender, and is broken out per construction (subject type, pronoun vs name). Source text is scraped Wikipedia/Gutenberg with Western-canon coverage bias; GECO's own no-significant-bias sanity check is cited as a bounded check, not as evidence of general representativeness.

For future XAI-evaluation practice the implication is concrete and actionable: a planted-ground-truth correctness benchmark should publish, alongside its attribution-mass score, a per-model per-token model-usage denominator U(m,t|s) measured by causal intervention on the model, and should report correctness per construction rather than as a single mean. Without that denominator, two benchmarks (or two fine-tuning depths of the same benchmark) that produce different rankings cannot be compared, because no one can tell whether the difference came from the explainers or from the models' shortcuts. Publishing the denominator turns an uninterpretable scalar into an identifiable, decomposable measurement -- which is what a benchmark for "correctness" must be to deserve the name.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

(a) IDENTIFIABILITY -- A and B are not separable without U. A low GECOBench score is AMBIGUOUS: either the explainer failed (error source A) or the model legitimately used non-planted cues and a faithful explanation went off-gold (error source B). With one scalar these are aliased. The fix introduces U(m,t|s) as the per-model per-token DENOMINATOR: dividing recovered mass by causally-usable mass makes A identifiable conditional on the model's mechanism, and Mech exposes B directly. Naive "just average mass on gold" cannot recover either. [provable-by-construction] that q(e,m,s) and Mech(m,s) are well-defined functions separating the terms; [conjectured->measured] that they differ materially across constructions.

(b) CIRCULARITY -- U is itself an attribution-like estimator. Measuring "what the model uses" with a saliency-flavored probe risks defining correctness in terms of another explainer. MITIGATION: the PRIMARY U signal is the counterfactual gender-SWAP (P1), which is the LABEL-DEFINING intervention -- flipping the gold token flips the human class by construction, so the causal handle does NOT depend on any saliency method. We further anchor with ORACLE-U on synthetic models where the data-generating usage is KNOWN, and report the estimator-vs-oracle gap. Ablation and IG-of-logit are used only as convergent cross-checks, never as the sole definition. [conjectured->measured] that the three U estimators agree and track oracle-U.

(c) CO-REFERENCE -- pronouns and co-referent gendered words ARE legitimate gender ground truth, not shortcuts. "Off-gold but used" must NOT blindly count them as model misalignment. We split OffUsed into Coref (w in G+(s)\G(s), legitimate) vs Shortcut (w not in G+(s)). This needs a reliable coref/gender-token pass; misclassification biases Mech. Difficulty is real but bounded: GECO sentences are short (<=30 tokens), single-subject, and gender lexicons are closed-class, so coref tagging is high-precision. [conjectured->measured] sensitivity of Mech to coref-tagging errors (we report it).

(d) SUBWORD/AGGREGATION CONFOUND IS CONSTRUCTION-CORRELATED -- swapped proper nouns split into many WordPiece shards; pronouns and common nouns often do not. So the tokenizer error Tok is entangled with subject-type, exactly the axis MMCC cares about. We measure Tok as its OWN component at word level (U is computed word-level to stay immune) and report Tok by subject-type. Naive word-pooling silently folds this into "explainer error." [conjectured->measured].

(e) CROSSED GLMM + MULTIPLE COMPARISONS -- explainer, model-mechanism, and construction are CROSSED (every explainer x every model x every cell), not nested, so a simple ANOVA is wrong; we need a crossed-random-effects GLMM, which is estimation-heavy and can have convergence/identifiability issues, plus many per-construction comparisons requiring multiplicity control (Benjamini-Hochberg). Tractable: modest factor counts (3 subject-types x a few distance/length bins x 4 depths), standard lme4/Bayesian fits, few GPU/CPU-hours. [conjectured->measured] variance shares.

(f) COLLAPSE MUST BE EXACT, NOT APPROXIMATE -- the whole credibility of "we generalize the benchmark" rests on P reducing to S_GECOBench identically under B1. An approximate collapse would mean P is a DIFFERENT metric, not a generalization. We therefore make collapse an algebraic identity (see Q5) and guard it with a < 1e-9 unit test on every (e,m). [provable-by-construction]; the test confirms the code matches the algebra.

Why still tractable overall: all heavy artifacts (data, checkpoints, attribution pipelines) are REUSED from GECOBench; the only new compute is O(L) intervention forwards per sentence + one GLMM fit; the hardest theoretical object (the collapse) is settled by construction, not by experiment; and the riskiest measurement (U) has a TRUE causal anchor (label-flipping swap) plus an oracle-U upper bound.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Three bodies of prior work are established but none fills the gap. (i) The suppressor-variable correctness program: Haufe et al. [1] (weights are not importance; pattern vs. filter), Wilming et al. [2] (linear ground-truth data isolating suppressors), Wilming, Kieslich, Clark and Haufe [3] (closed-form theory of XAI behavior under suppressors), XAI-TRIS [4] (non-linear synthetic image ground truth), and GECOBench [5] (the planted-gold NLP correctness benchmark itself). This program defines correctness and plants ground truth, but it scores attribution against the PLANTED gold and never against a measured per-model usage of that gold. (ii) The faithfulness/sanity literature: Jacovi and Goldberg [6] (faithfulness vs. plausibility), Adebayo et al. [7] (sanity checks), and Hooker et al. [8] (ROAR, a model-RETRAINING ablation). These critique explanations and even retrain models, but they neither plant token-level ground truth nor denominate a correctness score by per-token model usage. (iii) Causal interpretability: Vig et al. [9] (causal mediation analysis of gender behavior) and Meng et al. [10] (ROME causal tracing). These DO intervene on the model causally -- but to LOCATE where a mechanism lives (neurons, heads, MLP layers), not to denominate a correctness benchmark for attribution methods. Shortcut learning [18] supplies the motivation but offers no correctness estimator.

The novel cell, stated sharply: NO planted-ground-truth NLP correctness benchmark CONDITIONS its correctness score on a CAUSALLY-MEASURED per-model per-token ground-truth-USAGE denominator. Every existing planted benchmark scores attribution against the human prior (mass-on-planted-tokens) and therefore cannot tell, for any low score, whether it is penalizing an explainer that failed or a model that used a shortcut. MMCC names this conflation and the contribution closes it by a precise JOIN of two previously separate lines: suppressor-variable planted-gold correctness (lineage i) is multiplied through by a causal token-usage measurement built from the same intervention machinery used for mechanism localization (lineage iii), yielding a single decomposed correctness estimator. The join is what is new: causal tracing has never been used as the DENOMINATOR of a correctness benchmark, and the correctness benchmark has never carried a per-model usage denominator. The contribution is gender-agnostic and transfers unchanged to any swap-planted or template-planted correctness benchmark.

The contribution is a DIAGNOSTIC OF THE BENCHMARK, not a competitor. The promoted estimator is a per-construction, estimator-marginalized correctness PROFILE that separates explainer-attributable error from model-mechanism misalignment from tokenizer/aggregation error, denominated by U(m,t|s). It is a strict generalization of GECOBench: under the B1 identity it provably collapses back to GECOBench's exact scalar when measured usage U = 1 for every planted token, per-construction weights are uniform, a single fixed readout is used, and scoring is word-level with no subword split. Because the original benchmark is recovered as a special case, this improves GECOBench by making its hidden assumption (the model uses only the planted tokens) an explicit, measurable, and relaxable condition rather than a silent one.

What is NOT claimed. This work proposes no new attribution method -- IG, LRP, SHAP, Saliency, GradxInput/DeepLIFT, and pattern-based estimators are evaluated, not replaced. It does not resolve faithfulness in general; it makes operational, for the planted-gold correctness setting, the single distinction that a low correctness score may reflect model mechanism rather than explainer failure, and it gives the measurement (the causal usage denominator) needed to tell the two apart.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

THE CAUSAL GROUND-TRUTH USAGE DENOMINATOR U(m,t|s). U is an explainer-independent property of the model. It answers "does model m causally use planted word t to decide gender on sentence s?" Two model-side causal probes on the gender-class logit z(m,s):

(P1) Counterfactual gender swap. Let s' = swap(s,t) replace ONLY word t by its opposite-gender counterpart from GECO's paired lexicon, leaving all other tokens fixed. Define the swap effect E_swap(m,t|s) = |z_g(m,s) - z_g(m,s')| where z_g is the logit of the original gender class. A token the model truly uses produces large E_swap; an ignored token produces approximately 0. This is the label-defining intervention itself and does NOT depend on any attribution method.

(P2) Token ablation. Let s_ablate(t) replace pieces(t) by a gender-NEUTRAL baseline ([MASK], the natural MLM baseline for BERT [17]). Define E_ablate(m,t|s) = |z_g(m,s) - z_g(m, s_ablate(t))|. Ablation captures "remove the token's contribution" (presence sensitivity) as a complement to P1's sign-sensitive directional use.

Combine and normalize: E(m,t|s) = lambda x E_swap(m,t|s) + (1 - lambda) x E_ablate(m,t|s), lambda in [0,1] (default 0.5). U(m,t|s) = E(m,t|s) / max(E(m,t|s), Z_max(m,s)) where Z_max is the max single-word intervention effect over ALL content words on s, yielding U in [0,1] as t's causal pull relative to the strongest single-word lever. U is computed at WORD level (swap/ablate the whole word, all its subword pieces at once) so it is independent of the attribution pooling rule and does not inherit the tokenizer confound. U(m,t|s) is an internal mechanism diagnostic computed on the benchmark's existing fine-tuned model to separate explainer error from model shortcut; it is not, and is not proposed as, a deployed gender or demographic classifier.

PER-CONSTRUCTION CORRECTED CORRECTNESS P(e,m,c). Per sentence, define normalized non-negative attribution shares: r(e,m,s,w) = relu(A(e,m,s,w)) / SUM_{w' in s} relu(A(e,m,s,w')), so SUM_w r = 1. The corrected per-sentence correctness is q(e,m,s) = [SUM_{t in G(s)} U(m,t|s) x r(e,m,s,t)] / [SUM_{t in G(s)} U(m,t|s)]: the explainer is scored on hitting planted tokens IN PROPORTION to how much the model causally uses them. If U=0 for a planted token, missing it costs the explainer nothing; if U=1, hitting it is mandatory.

MODEL-MECHANISM TERM (Mech). Mech(m,s) = SUM_{w not in G(s)} U(m,w|s) / max(Z, SUM_{w in s} U(m,w|s)). Mech is explainer-independent: it is the share of the model's causal usage that lies off the planted gold (high Mech = shortcut-prone construction). Co-referent pronouns that also carry gender are split into Coref (legitimate off-subject gold the model uses) vs Shortcut (genuine shortcut: context, position, register, subword shards of proper nouns). The per-construction profile aggregates: P_corr(e,m,c), P_mech(m,c), P_coref(e,m,c), P_short(e,m,c). Estimator marginalization over a readout set R = {sign convention, normalization, integration steps}: P(e,m,c) = E_{rho in R}[P_corr(e,m,c; rho)], with readout-induced variance as a stability diagnostic.

TOKENIZER TERM (Tok). Tok(e,m,c; AGG) = P_word-level(e,m,c) - P_subword(e,m,c; AGG), AGG in {sum, mean, max}. Tok isolates the error injected purely by tokenization + pooling; its construction-correlation (names split, pronouns do not) is itself a finding. Tok = 0 by construction when AGG = sum and gold is single-piece (the B1-iv regime) [provable-by-construction]; Tok is large and subject-type-correlated for swapped proper nouns [conjectured->measured].

EXACT B1 COLLAPSE IDENTITY [provable-by-construction]. Under four conditions -- (B1-i) U(m,t|s) = 1 for every planted token t in G(s); (B1-ii) uniform per-construction weights omega(c) = omega_GB(c); (B1-iii) single fixed readout R = {r_GB} with GECOBench's sign/normalization; (B1-iv) word-level scoring, AGG = sum, every gold word is single-piece -- the promoted estimator collapses EXACTLY: P(e,m) = S_GECOBench(e,m) within floating-point tolerance. The unit test asserts |P_collapsed(e,m) - S_GECOBench(e,m)| < 1e-9 for every (e,m). This is an algebraic identity, not an empirical fit [provable-by-construction].

DATASETS. Primary: GECO/GECOBench [5] (published gender-controlled sentences, train/test splits, four fine-tuning-depth checkpoints). No regeneration of data; only new compute is the causal-usage interventions + GLMM. Transfer 1 (SST-swap-planted): template/swap-planted polarity set to test MMCC outside gender, clearly labeled as honestly constructed. Transfer 2 (synthetic suppressor-planted text): controlled set in the Wilming/Haufe [2] lineage with a planted suppressor word, providing an oracle-U upper bound, clearly labeled as constructed.

MODELS. BERT-base depth ladder (GECOBench checkpoints): the primary subjects. Oracle/linear-probe model (placebo Leg-1): decision is by construction a linear probe on ONLY the planted gold token embedding, so U(gold)=1, U(non-gold)=0 by construction, driving Mech -> 0. Shortcut-prone stress model: BERT fine-tuned with the subject token MASKED during training, forcing reliance on context/co-reference, yielding high Mech by construction.

ATTRIBUTION METHODS SCORED. Integrated Gradients [11], LRP [12], Saliency [14], GradxInput/DeepLIFT [15], SHAP [13], pattern-based [16]; plus random/uniform explainer (placebo Leg-2: attribution drawn independent of the model). All run through the same readout-marginalization R.

METRICS. (1) P(e,m,c) profile with decomposition {P_corr, P_mech, P_coref, P_short}. (2) U distributions per construction (histograms/means). (3) B1 collapse-fidelity error |P_collapsed - S_GECOBench| < 1e-9 [provable-by-construction]. (4) GLMM variance-component shares (explainer/model-mechanism/construction/interaction) [conjectured->measured]. (5) Leaderboard RANK-CHANGE: Kendall-tau of method rankings before (S_GECOBench) vs after (MMCC-corrected P) [conjectured->measured].

BASELINES. B1: original GECOBench scalar (collapse target). B2: oracle-model placebo (Leg-1, P_mech approximately 0, clean explainer-only signal). B3: random-explainer placebo (Leg-2, P_corr minimized, Mech invariant). B4: full MMCC profile with decomposition.

ABLATIONS. (1) B1 exact-collapse unit test [provable-by-construction]. (2) Dual-placebo legs: Leg-1 zeros Mech; Leg-2 leaves Mech unchanged (numerical invariance test that Mech is explainer-free). (3) Per-construction factorial: subject-type x context-distance-bin x length-bin x depth. (4) Subword-aggregation sensitivity: AGG in {sum, mean, max}; report Tok and its subject-type correlation. (5) U-estimator robustness: compare U from swap (P1), ablation (P2), IG-of-logit; report oracle-U upper bound and estimator-vs-oracle gap; lambda and baseline-token sensitivity. (6) Fine-tuning-depth dose-response: does P_mech fall monotonically as depth increases (deep fine-tunes localize on subject; shallow lean on shortcut/context)? [conjectured->measured], directional and falsifiable.

FALSIFIABILITY TRIAD. (i) By-construction B1 collapse (exact, refutable if error > tolerance). (ii) Dual placebo (refutable if Leg-1 fails to zero Mech or Leg-2 moves Mech). (iii) Dose-response monotonicity (refutable if Mech does not decline with depth) [conjectured->measured].

EXPECTED OUTCOMES. The headline result is leaderboard RANK-CHANGE: if the ranking of IG, LRP, Saliency, SHAP, DeepLIFT, GradxInput, and pattern-based methods changes after MMCC correction, then published correctness rankings of attribution methods were partly rankings of per-construction model-shortcut prevalence, not explainer quality [conjectured->measured]. The B1 collapse confirms the promoted estimator is a strict generalization, not a different metric. The dose-response test provides a mechanistic account: shallow fine-tunes yield high Mech (model uses shortcuts), producing the largest leaderboard distortion, while deep fine-tunes yield low Mech (model localizes on planted token), recovering GECOBench's ranking as a special case [conjectured->measured]. The GLMM apportions correctness variance into explainer, model-mechanism, construction, and interaction shares, quantifying how much of the benchmarked "quality" is actually model artifact [conjectured->measured].

COMPUTE ENVELOPE. Explicitly modest: reuse GECOBench checkpoints + sentences. New cost = intervention forward passes (per sentence: 1 base + one swap + one ablation per content word, approximately O(L) forwards with L<=30) over the test set, plus attribution runs already standard in GECOBench, plus one crossed GLMM fit. Total: a few GPU-hours on BERT-base; no large-scale training.

References

[1] S. Haufe, F. Meinecke, K. Goergen, S. Daehne, J.-D. Haynes, B. Blankertz, F. Biessmann. "On the interpretation of weight vectors of linear models in multivariate neuroimaging." NeuroImage, 2014.

[2] R. Wilming, C. Budding, K.-R. Mueller, S. Haufe. "Scrutinizing XAI using linear ground-truth data with suppressor variables." Machine Learning (Springer), 2022. arXiv:2111.07473.

[3] R. Wilming, L. Kieslich, B. Clark, S. Haufe. "Theoretical Behavior of XAI Methods in the Presence of Suppressor Variables." ICML, 2023. arXiv:2306.01464.

[4] B. Clark, R. Wilming, S. Haufe. "XAI-TRIS: Non-linear image benchmarks to quantify false positive post-hoc attribution of feature importance." 2023. arXiv:2306.12816.

[5] R. Wilming, A. Dox, H. Schulz, M. Oliveira, B. Clark, S. Haufe. "GECOBench: A Gender-Controlled Text Dataset and Benchmark for Quantifying Biases in Explanations." Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence, 2024/2025. arXiv:2406.11547.

[6] A. Jacovi, Y. Goldberg. "Towards Faithfully Interpretable NLP Systems: How should we define and evaluate faithfulness?" ACL, 2020. arXiv:2004.03685.

[7] J. Adebayo, J. Gilmer, M. Muelly, I. Goodfellow, M. Hardt, B. Kim. "Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps." NeurIPS, 2018. arXiv:1810.03292.

[8] S. Hooker, D. Erhan, P.-J. Kindermans, B. Kim. "A Benchmark for Interpretability Methods in Deep Neural Networks (ROAR)." NeurIPS, 2019. arXiv:1806.10758.

[9] J. Vig, S. Gehrmann, Y. Belinkov, S. Qian, D. Nevo, S. Sakenis, J. Huang, Y. Singer, S. Shieber. "Causal Mediation Analysis for Interpreting Neural NLP: The Case of Gender Bias." NeurIPS, 2020. arXiv:2004.12265.

[10] K. Meng, D. Bau, A. Andonian, Y. Belinkov. "Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT (ROME)." NeurIPS, 2022. arXiv:2202.05262.

[11] M. Sundararajan, A. Taly, Q. Yan. "Axiomatic Attribution for Deep Networks (Integrated Gradients)." ICML, 2017. arXiv:1703.01365.

[12] S. Bach, A. Binder, G. Montavon, F. Klauschen, K.-R. Mueller, W. Samek. "On Pixel-Wise Explanations for Non-Linear Classifier Decisions by Layer-Wise Relevance Propagation (LRP)." PLOS ONE, 2015.

[13] S. Lundberg, S.-I. Lee. "A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions (SHAP)." NeurIPS, 2017. arXiv:1705.07874.

[14] K. Simonyan, A. Vedaldi, A. Zisserman. "Deep Inside Convolutional Networks: Visualising Image Classification Models and Saliency Maps." ICLR Workshop, 2014. arXiv:1312.6034.

[15] A. Shrikumar, P. Greenside, A. Kundaje. "Learning Important Features Through Propagating Activation Differences (DeepLIFT)." ICML, 2017. arXiv:1704.02685.

[16] P.-J. Kindermans, K. T. Schuett, M. Alber, K.-R. Mueller, D. Erhan, B. Kim, S. Daehne. "Learning how to explain neural networks: PatternNet and PatternAttribution." ICLR, 2018. arXiv:1705.05598.

[17] J. Devlin, M.-W. Chang, K. Lee, K. Toutanova. "BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding." NAACL, 2019. arXiv:1810.04805.

[18] R. Geirhos, J.-H. Jacobsen, C. Michaelis, R. Zemel, W. Brendel, M. Bethge, F. A. Wichmann. "Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks." Nature Machine Intelligence, 2020. arXiv:2004.07780.
